The only time that I won't do the SVs from this direction would be, for example, if a patient has had an LAR or for some reason has tons of adhesions down in the cul-de-sac and I don't want to have to work on the bowels and pull the bowels out and try to get into this plane, then I would do the SVs the old-fashioned way, you know, from dropping the bladder or coming through the bladder neck and then do them anteriorly.
But that's obviously a very rare scenario, so I would say probably Since I started doing it this direction, I've maybe had to do it two or three times the other direction.
But I think it's good to know both. It's good to be comfortable with both.
One thing that people, I think, get a little bit nervous about from this direction if they're not used to it is they worry about injuring the ureter.
I didn't make a big wide incision. The ureters would be way out here. So as long as you keep your incision just narrow enough to do the dissection, there's really no need to worry about the ureters.
Honestly, I've never really seen the ureters back here in this direction because we stay, you know, medial. We stay in the midline.

And some people will try to look for the vas back here, starting out laterally towards the inguinal ring and then following the vas down into this cul-de-sac area. And I recommend against that. I know that's how it was done in the laparoscopic days, but, you know, if you start dissecting

The anatomy is very, very consistent. If you just make the incision one centimeter above that line where the yellow meets the white, stay in the hay and just go down to the denonviasis.

Actually, what I would say is that the thing that people really, I think, struggle with early on when they're first starting this approach is just finding the SV and the VAS, especially on an obese patient because it may be deep in there.

So I think what you should do, a good way of kind of transitioning if you want to try it is, you know, go back there, come back here into the cul-de-sac, try to find the anatomy. If you find the anatomy, go as far as you can, and then when you start to struggle, go back to what you're used to.
You haven't lost anything. So I would say just kind of ease your way into it. Don't force it.

You know, if you get back in the cul-de-sac and you're struggling and you can't find the anatomy, then just go back to what you're used to and try on the next prostate. The other thing is we're doing this case on a pneumo of six. So this is my routine. We've been doing this now for about five hundred cases. We do the prostates all at a pneumo of six, really all pelvic surgery, so bladders as well as cystectomies.

We do them at a pneumo of six. And so you'll notice here that things will seem like they're not quite as stretched out as what you're used to when you use a pneumo of ten, fifteen, twelve, whatever you usually use.
So you wouldn't have to do that, of course. I mean, if you were doing it. Are you using the air seal system with that? Yes.
Yeah, we're using air seal at six, exactly. So what we found is, you know, my fellow just presented our comparison of three hundred at six versus three hundred at fifteen, what we used to do.
And what we found is that, you know, the The air seal allows you to do this ultra-low pressure without losing pneumo every time the assistant sucks.

But just the fact that you're able to do it at a pneumo of six is really quite helpful in several ways. One is that the patient has less peritoneal stretch, so it seems like the patients tend to have less pain.

And that was what our data showed. Our pain scores were less at the pneumo of six. And then the shoulder pain that people always get after laparoscopic surgery, it really eliminated it. We just didn't see it anymore.

We don't see that shoulder pain That people always complain about after you know, we still see with our kidneys because we don't do them at six But really on the prostates we don't see it We also don't see that scrotal swelling that we used to see when we did it at higher pneumo pressure So that pneumo of six really has solved several problems for us So we've really made it our routine again.

We've done it for about five hundred cases, you know consecutive cases We've not had to you know, adjust it regardless of obesity or anything And then the other thing is some people might ask, well, do you get more bleeding at a pneumo of six, you know, venous bleeding?

And the answer is really no, not a clinically significant amount. You know, we've not transfused any patient yet who we've done at six. So, you know, hundreds of patients later, we've never had to transfuse anybody. And, you know, really what I like about it is that if it's going to bleed post-operatively, it's going to bleed at six.

So when I leave the operating room, and there's no bleeding and things are dry, then I'm not worried that when the patient's in recovery and on the floor with a pneumo of zero that they might bleed because I was looking at it at six, and if they're going to bleed at six, they're going to bleed at zero. If they're not bleeding at six, they're probably not going to bleed at zero.

But if you have a pneumo of twelve or fifteen, well, then, you know, you might worry postoperatively that some big vein was kind of let loose and might cause trouble. So I really think from a bleeding point of view, I'm not worried about the ultra low pressure. If anything, I think it's helping us.

I know at our institution, the anesthesiologists have been extremely happy with us switching to air seal and lowering our pressures. We've been doing it about at ten, but after seeing your fellows' data, I want to go even lower, push that limit. Are your anesthesiologists pretty excited about it, Phil?

Yeah, I mean, like you said, they noticed a huge difference when we went to air seal number one, but then there have been patients that I've had where the pneumo of six was enabling, meaning that the patient would not have tolerated a high pneumo pressure either because of COPD or just very, very obese patients.

We wouldn't have been able to do the cases at a pneumo, you know, up ten or fifteen, but doing them at six was enabling for us to be able to offer them you know, surgery as opposed to radiation. So, you know, and also for the cystectomy patients, as you know, Clayton, these guys are all heavy smokers.

So they've got COPD, ninety percent of the time. And sometimes their COPD is so bad that you don't even know if you're going to be able to get them through surgery. But I think the robotic approach is huge.
They do so much better postoperatively, but just getting them through the operation with the pneumo peritoneum and the Trendelenburg, the pneumo of six again has been enabling in more than one case.

but I routinely do it because I think it helps me to see the contour of the gland so that when I'm doing the bladder neck, I can really tell where the prostate is turning so that I can see where the bladder neck is going to be a little bit better.

So that's kind of my routine is to go ahead and open the endopelvic fascia before tackling the bladder neck. And then some people will pre-place their DVC stitch at this point. And, you know, again, I used to do that, you know, probably the first couple thousand prostates,

would pre-place the DVC stitch. And then I stopped doing that. Now I just cut it and I over-sew it. So, you know, for the last fifteen hundred, two thousand, however many, we don't pre-place the DVC stitch anymore.
The surgeon proceeds through the bladder neck during the prostatectomy. At the end of the procedure, during the apical dissection, the dorsal venous complex (DVC) is cut cold and then over-sewn.

And you'll be surprised. You would think that it would bleed like a stuck pig with the pneumo at six. But actually, we noticed no difference. We used to turn up the pneumo just for that step, just when we come through the DVC because we're cutting it cold.

We'd say, okay, well, let's turn up the pneumo for that because we don't want to be at six. But then what we found out was that actually makes no difference, whether it was at six or whether it was at ten, fifteen, or even twenty. It really didn't make any difference. It didn't seem to bleed anymore. It does bleed, but it's not terrible.

Okay, let's have a wipe before we take the bladder neck on. So a couple of tips before we start the bladder neck and please interrupt me if there's any questions, obviously, but a couple of tips before we tackle the bladder neck.

One is that you don't want your pneumo, your cautery setting to be too high. You know, I purposely keep the cautery setting low. So again, on the Irby, we're doing a monopolar of four and on the, if we're using the regular bovie, the force triad, we're using the cautery at twenty-five. So this is now at twenty-five.
The surgeon carefully dissects through the bladder neck, revealing distinct muscle fibers that are clearly visible and differentiated from the prostate tissue.

And so that's something that you lose if you turn your cautery up too much. Some people they turn up their cautery thinking they'll get less bleeding, they'll be able to see better, but actually you see worse when your cautery is too high because you lose the distinction of the muscle fibers.

And the other thing too, the other tip that I would give is that I don't like to see, you know, when my fellows are doing this, I don't like to see them digging the scissor into the tissue. You just got to float above it. You're just touching it with the cautery and just floating above it and letting the muscle fibers contract away. And that's how you can tell that you're in the right place.

So there's no digging. If you dig, you'll be more likely to accidentally get into the prostate. So those are my tips is just keep the cautery setting low and then just float. And then make sure your left hand is giving you backwards traction. That's very critical is that the left hand is just as active as the right hand when you're doing the bladder neck.

The surgeon uses the left hand to apply backward traction on the bladder neck, stretching the tissue to clearly define the muscle fibers while avoiding entry into the prostate. The right hand performs precise cutting actions.

Any other tips you would add to that Clayton? No, I think it's a, I think you're doing a great job. I think, you know, that people underscore that left hand and I can, you know, if they did, they can't see it because it's kind of off the screen partially, but you're, you can see that you're actively pulling back a little bit or just leaning, leaning back. That's a better way to put it.
And you can side to side to get that traction as you go. That's a great job. Yeah. All robotic surgery is two handed. You can't operate with one hand. Sometimes people,

you'll see the left hand is just kind of floating in the breeze, doing nothing. And they're trying to do everything with one hand. And that's, I think early on, you know, that's kind of, I think people concentrate so hard on what their right hand is doing. They forget that they even have the left hand, but I think with experience, you find that the left hand really makes the operation go so much smoother.
Kind of like the fourth arm management too, is the same thing. As you get more experienced with it, it makes the operation go smoother. Yeah, it looks really nice, Ronnie. You want to comment?

Some people actually, like I see you're kind of working in like wide, wide circles. Some people like to tunnel straight down and, you know, straight down and then work their way out lateral. Do you find any advantage to either one?

Yeah, absolutely. That's a great point, actually. So the people who do that, the people who kind of go straight down in the middle until they see the catheter are really the most expert surgeons. I mean, they're phenomenal surgeons who do that. And they're able to keep themselves from getting into the prostate just by experience.

But I tell my fellows and I tell people who are less, you know, do less, either less experienced or do lower volume robotic surgery. They're not doing it as often. I think this technique is a safer way because, again, you maintain the contour of the prostate. You use the contour of the prostate to make sure you're in the right place. So as I'm going out, I'm looking at the contour of the prostate and I'm asking myself, okay, where does this prostate go?

Sometimes you'll be surprised. Sometimes you won't realize it. You may think, okay, the urethra is going to be here, but actually no, it's further back or it's closer. So for me, you know, I really prefer to do this technique of kind of keeping it flat. I'm just trying to keep it flat and I'm going back and forth, trying to stay at the same plane with my dissection until I get down to urethra.

Once I'm close to urethra, then I'll dig a little bit down towards urethra. But I don't want to be working in a hole too much because then you can get fooled. Especially, again, if you're not the most experienced or you're just kind of a more lower volume surgeon, you can really get fooled and you can end up somewhere in the middle of the prostate. I've seen it happen before.

And, you know, that's why I try to stay out of a hole. But, you know, again, there's different ways of doing it and there's different things that work for different people. There's phenomenal surgeons who do it that way. That's kind of the Henry Ford way, actually. So that was the way that I, you know, kind of, you know, learned how to do it. And when you see people doing that, they're usually people who trained at Henry Ford. So there's nothing wrong with that method.

But again, I don't recommend it, though, for surgeons who maybe are not quite as expertise, don't have the same expertise as the Henry Ford guys do. It looks very nice, Ronnie. I think we're going to do a clean here for you.

So it looks really good. Yeah, absolutely. That's a great point.

I like to spare the size of the bladder neck as much as possible and not do a reconstruction at the end. I don't think it makes a difference to continence. If you look at the data from those who do bladder neck sparing versus those that don't, it doesn't seem to make a difference for continence to spare the bladder neck.

But I do it more for convenience sake because I think it makes the anastomosis easier and less likely for me to get a leak afterwards. If I spare the bladder neck and I'm sewing two things that are about the same size together, the bladder neck's about the same size as the urethra, then I'm going to get a nice smooth anastomosis. If there's a big mismatch in the size, it's hard to get a watertight closure as well.
That's right. Explain. When you do have a big bladder neck, do you use a fish mouth reconstruction or do you just do like a, just close it anteriorly and do you use a V-lock for that?

So I don't, I do, I do a three and a nine o'clock stitch, just a figure of eight at three and nine o'clock. And but I very, very rarely do it. Yeah, another point I would make for those who are not aware is that, you know, when you use the scissor, again, if you're kind of a newer user of the robot, when you use the cautery

if you want to coag something, then use both tips. If you want to just be more cutting, you can just use one tip. So less char, in other words. So that's why I'm using one tip to cut. But if I see a bleeder, I'll go to like this and I'll use both tips. It's going to give you more char, though. So it's sort of like the difference between a needle tip bovie and a regular bovie. It just concentrates the current more when you use one prong of the scissor, as opposed to both.

When you use both, you're going to get more coagging and more charring. So that's why I like to cut with one prong, which I think, you know, most people do as well. So Ronnie, this is Jay here. But again, you don't want to be too close to the prostate when you do that. You want to be a little bit further back from that point. Ronnie, so if you were in the event to have like a buttonhole situation here, how do you repair that?

Do you repair that immediately when you identify it or do you wait until your anastomosis? So I've only had that happen once so far. And I think the key is prevention. The key to preventing, you know, a buttonhole is, you know, again, all the things that we're talking about, the left hand giving you traction,

One thing that you didn't see me doing on this case that, you know, is something that you should really think about doing when you're doing the posterior bladder neck is every so often you can go and look inside here and figure out what direction is the bladder going, what direction is the prostate going here. Because sometimes what will happen is that the bladder will go way under the prostate. You'll have like a big jutting lobe here, and the bladder will go way under. And that's the time when you buttonhole because you're coming straight down thinking that the bladder is like this one where it was a shelf. but actually the bladder is going down like this, and then you make a hole in the backside of the bladder.
